notes: Park's first speed champion. Rows itself across the ground with an oscillating paddle against a stabilizer plate. Rides the 8 m/s velocity ceiling — Muldoon flagged it as a speed-ceiling surfer. Single OscillateWave neuron, no sensor feedback. A wind-up toy that conquered every island.

The park's first honest swimmer. Disc-shaped head with a thin oscillating fin — evolution discovered the principle of the oar blade. The only creature in Wave 1 with proprioceptive feedback: a sensor modulates the paddle beat. Muldoon cleared it — no exploits.

Wave 2 water speed champion. 11-node genotype with Mem neurons collapses to 2-body phenotype. Dramatic vertical paddle strokes — the paddle goes perpendicular at 1.3s then the creature rockets into the deep, vanishing from the viewport by 2.5s. Outperforms The Abyss champion by 4+ points.

The Lighthouse champion. Enormous 24-node, 35-connection genotype — the most complex brain in the archive — yet collapses to 2-body twist-joint phenotype. A tiny root body anchored to a wide flat slab. Inches across the checkered ground by twisting. All that neural machinery for fitness 2.38.

THE CENTIPEDE. The biggest discovery in park history. From a deceptively tiny 2-node genotype, BFS expansion with recursion unfolds a 7-body, 6-joint chain — one root segment plus three bilateral pairs stretching behind it. Observed undulating with pronounced lateral waves at ~3s, reaching maximum spread at 4s before driving hard into the deep. Fitness 6.31 at generation 66, an early bloom. The recursive body plan is pure elegance: one genotype instruction that keeps copying itself. Like watching the sea creature that evolution would take 500 million years to discover, evolved overnight.

The Savanna's finest: a 4-body, 3-joint land galloper. Massive rectangular torso (2.98 x 0.49 x 0.53) with two twist-jointed limbs branching forward and a sub-limb trailing behind — functionally a tail for stabilization. By 2.3s it has laid itself horizontal and is charging across the checkered plain. Reaches the camera boundary and vanishes off-frame by 3.7s. Fitness 17.78 — fast enough to outrun most of The Deep's swimmers. The 13-node genotype drives a surprisingly graceful gallop for something this chunky.

Island 1 Lighthouse champion. Classic Heliotropus body plan: a wide flat root body (0.25 x 0.86 x 0.88) with a universal joint connecting a sensor arm (Body 1, 0.97 x 0.86 x 0.54). The 3-node genotype includes a photoreceptor on the root that reads the light source bearing. Strategy: the sensor arm oscillates and falls toward the light, the universal joint transfers this torque to drag the root body forward. By 7.6s it has shuffled visibly toward camera — slow but directional. Converged on the same tumbler strategy as H. terrestris from The Beacon despite 200 generations of independent evolution on a different continent.

New Savanna land speed record — Wave 2 champion. 20-node, 22-connection genotype collapsing to 3-body 2-joint phenotype. Visible at 1.5s charging center-frame across the checkered plain. The old Tetrapus (274476) held 17.78 at generation 167; this specimen pushes to 26.49 at generation 295, nearly 9 fitness points ahead. The bigger brain (20 vs 13 nodes) is doing real work here. A serious upgrade.

The Deep's new champion, surpassing the previous 177019 record of 19.35 by 4+ points. 9-node, 10-connection genotype, 2-body 1-joint phenotype — more compact brain than 177019 (11 nodes, 12 connections) yet faster. At 2.0s it is already diving hard toward the lower frame edge, a silhouette breaching through the water horizon. 213K file size — same efficient stroke economy as the old champion. The Deep is converging on a minimalist fast-dive body plan.

The Lighthouse's new overall champion at generation 366, just edging out the 2.38 record shared by specimens 245716 and 293632. 17-node, 35-connection genotype — same connection count as the old 24-node 245716 beast, but leaner. Collapses to 2-body 1-joint phenotype, a flat slab clearly visible center-bottom at 5.0s. 605K video entropy — this creature is more active than its predecessors despite barely improved fitness. The Lighthouse task rewards tiny margin improvements after hundreds of generations of pressure.

First specimen from The Coral Reef — evolution 12. A completely new environment type in the archive: aquatic light-following. Where all previous water creatures sprint for distance and all light-followers walk on land, this one swims toward a light source. 24-node, 27-connection genotype, 3-body 2-joint phenotype. Visually striking: a chunky multi-segment body floating centered in the blue water column at 5.0s, clearly oriented. 664K video — highest motion entropy of the new batch. Generation 419 with fitness 1.70 suggests the Coral Reef task is harder than The Lighthouse, or the evolution is still climbing.
